data: advanced-strategy-application
Advanced Strategy Application
Advanced / Application
Make expert-level instructional and problem-solving decisions under realistic constraints.
asa-1
multiple-choice
A cohort has high completion but low delayed retention. Which intervention best targets the gap?
Add delayed retrieval and spaced review after the initial quiz
Make the quiz shorter but never revisit the content
Remove explanations to increase speed
Only celebrate completion and avoid measuring retention
The weak outcome is retention after time passes.
Delayed retrieval and spaced review directly target durable retention. For further study, review retrieval practice, spacing, and delayed post-tests.
asa-2
A learner misses scenario items because they choose the first familiar term they see. Which design change best helps?
Add prompts that require identifying the relevant constraint before answering
Add more familiar terms without context
Remove scenarios entirely
Make every answer choice a synonym
The issue is premature matching, not lack of vocabulary alone.
Identifying constraints slows pattern matching and focuses attention on what the scenario actually requires. For further study, review problem representation and expert-novice differences.
asa-3
true-false
Adaptive practice should use evidence from learner performance to decide what comes next.
Adaptive means responsive.
Adaptive practice changes difficulty, content, or support based on performance evidence. For further study, review mastery learning and adaptive sequencing.
asa-4
A team must reduce quiz length without losing coverage of a complex objective. Which approach is strongest?
Use fewer, richer scenarios that each require multiple targeted reasoning steps
Delete every difficult question first
Keep only questions that assess the same definition
Remove explanations and leave all items unchanged
Coverage can come from carefully designed tasks, not only item count.
Richer scenarios can preserve construct coverage while reducing item count. For further study, review assessment blueprints and evidence-centered design.
asa-5
fill-blank
A plan that maps objectives to question types and coverage is an assessment _____.
blueprint
It is an architectural plan for the assessment.
An assessment blueprint maps objectives, content, and item types to planned evidence. For further study, review test blueprints and table-of-specifications methods.
asa-6
A quiz is intended for learners using screen readers. Which content decision best supports accessibility?
Write prompts and explanations that do not depend on visual position alone
Use 'the green thing on the left' as the only clue
Put all meaning in color differences
Remove text labels from answer choices
Meaning should survive when visual layout is not available.
Accessible content should communicate meaning through text and structure, not only visual placement or color. For further study, review inclusive assessment design and WCAG text alternatives.
asa-7
A learner succeeds only when hints are visible. What is the best next step if the goal is independent performance?
Fade hints gradually while monitoring accuracy
Remove all practice opportunities
Keep hints permanent and stop measuring independence
Increase unrelated visual detail
The support should decrease without dropping the learner off a cliff.
Fading hints lets learners practice independence while preserving enough support to keep improving. For further study, review scaffolding and fading.
asa-8
When a quiz has a high-stakes purpose, item explanations are still useful for learning but may need to be separated from score reporting.
Think about learning feedback versus assessment security.
High-stakes contexts may require different timing or access rules for explanations, while still valuing feedback for later learning. For further study, review formative versus summative assessment.
asa-9
A team sees that learners miss questions with long prompts even when the concept is familiar. What should they investigate first?
Whether reading load or irrelevant detail is masking the target skill
Whether the concept should be deleted entirely
Whether all learners dislike quizzes
Whether shorter answer IDs would change learning
Long prompts can create demands unrelated to the target concept.
Prompt length and irrelevant detail may add extraneous load that interferes with performance. For further study, review cognitive load and item clarity.
asa-10
A course wants learners to choose among similar strategies. Which practice format best matches the goal?
Interleaved scenarios where learners must decide which strategy applies
Blocked drills that label the strategy in every prompt
Rereading the strategy list only
A single true/false question about strategy names
The goal includes selecting a strategy, not just executing a named one.
Interleaved scenarios require learners to discriminate among strategies, matching the decision skill. For further study, review interleaving and discrimination learning.
asa-11
A small check during learning that guides the next teaching move is a formative _____.
assessment
It is assessment used while learning is still in progress.
Formative assessment provides evidence that can shape instruction before final evaluation. For further study, review formative assessment cycles.
asa-12
A learner asks why an answer is wrong. Which feedback is most actionable?
Name the misconception, contrast it with the correct rule, and suggest a next practice step
Say only 'wrong' and move on
Reveal the score but hide the answer
Give a compliment unrelated to the response
Actionable feedback helps the learner change the next attempt.
Specific feedback that addresses the misconception and next step is more useful than a judgment alone. For further study, review feedback specificity and error correction.
asa-13
A scenario can assess multiple objectives, but the explanation should make clear which reasoning steps mattered.
Complex items need transparent reasoning after the answer.
When scenarios combine objectives, explanations should unpack the key reasoning steps to support learning. For further study, review complex item design and rationale writing.
asa-14
A team wants to personalize review without overwhelming learners. Which rule is most defensible?
Prioritize concepts that are important, recently missed, and prerequisites for later work
Always assign the longest possible review set
Only review topics the learner never misses
Randomize everything and ignore performance data
Personalization should consider importance, evidence, and sequence.
Important, recently missed prerequisite concepts are high-value targets for review. For further study, review adaptive practice policies and prerequisite mapping.
